task.title: Exercise 3: Writing XML Schemas (XSD)
task.shortdesc: Practice writing XSD schemas with data types, restrictions, and complex types — then compare XSD validation with DTD validation to understand the differences.

ul.li: Completed Exercise 2 (DTD Validation)
Read Part 3: Chapters 8-10 (What Is XSD, Elements and Types, Restrictions and Patterns)
An XML editor that supports XSD validation

context.p: You will re-create the BookVault catalog schema using XSD — experiencing the additional power XSD provides over DTDs. Then you will write schemas for new document types.

ul.li: Root element: catalog containing one or more book elements
Each book has children: title (string), author (string, 1 to 5 occurrences), year (integer), price (decimal), optional description (string)
book has attributes: isbn (string, required), category (enumeration: fiction/non-fiction/technical, required)
price has attribute: currency (string, default "USD")

stepresult.p: Your XSD should be a well-formed XML document with <xs:schema> as the root.

step.cmd: Create xml-exercises/catalog-xsd.xml that validates against your XSD.

info.p: Use xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation to link to your schema:
Include at least 3 books with correct data types in all elements.

stepresult.p: The document should validate without errors.

step.cmd: Test XSD data type validation by introducing type errors.

info.p: Try each change and observe what happens:

ol.li: Put text in the <year> element (declared as integer): <year>twenty twenty-five</year>
Put a negative number in <price> — does it validate? (It should, because you declared it as xs:decimal, not a restricted type)

info.note: Notice how XSD catches data type errors that a DTD would miss. A DTD treats all content as text, so <year>hello</year> would be valid against a DTD but invalid against an XSD.

step.cmd: Enhance your XSD with custom restricted types.

ul.li: priceType — decimal with minimum value of 0.00 and maximum of 9999.99, with exactly 2 fraction digits
yearType — integer between 1900 and 2100
isbnType — string matching the pattern [0-9]{3}-[0-9]-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{6}-[0-9]

info.p: Replace the built-in types with your custom types and re-validate your XML.

stepresult.p: Now a <year>1800</year> or <price>-5.00</price> should fail validation.

step.cmd: Create a new schema for a different domain: xml-exercises/employees.xsd

ul.li: Define a named complex type addressType with: street, city, state, zip
Define a named complex type personType with: firstName, lastName, email (anyURI type)
Define employeeType by extending personType with: department, hireDate (date), address (addressType)
Root element: company with attribute name, containing one or more employee elements

info.p: Create a matching xml-exercises/employees.xml with sample data.

step.cmd: Reflect on DTD vs. XSD differences.

info.p: Write down answers to these questions:

ul.li: What validation errors did XSD catch that DTD could not? Give specific examples.
Which schema language was easier to write? Why?
When would you choose DTD over XSD?
Why does DITA provide both DTD and XSD grammars?

p.b: Self-Check — you should now be able to:

ul.li: ✓ Write XSD schemas with simple and complex types
✓ Use built-in data types: string, integer, decimal, date, boolean
✓ Create custom types with restrictions: enumerations, patterns, ranges
✓ Control element occurrences with minOccurs/maxOccurs
✓ Define named complex types and reuse them
✓ Extend complex types (type inheritance)
✓ Link XML documents to XSD schemas and validate
✓ Explain the key differences between DTD and XSD
